quedar.description: to stay, meet up, fit, be left

usecases.name: Meet up / make plans
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedar con alguien' to say you're meeting up with a person. This is the everyday informal way to talk about hanging out with friends — much more natural than 'reunirse' in casual speech.

demo.context: A: Did you see Sofía last night?
demo.native: Yeah, I met up with her for coffee.
demo.spanish: Sí, quedé con ella para tomar un café.

practice.context: Adam: Are you doing anything this Saturday?
practice.english: Let's meet up at the bar.

usecases.name: Agree on a plan
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedar en' + noun or infinitive to say two people agreed on something or settled on a plan. It's like 'we decided on' or 'we settled that' — the result of coming to an agreement together.

demo.context: A: Did you figure out a plan?
demo.native: We agreed to meet at noon.

practice.context: Adam: What did you two decide in the end?
practice.english: We agreed to call each other.

usecases.name: Be left / remain
usecases.explanation: When something remains or is left over, use 'quedar' like a reverse verb — the thing that's left is the subject. 'Me quedan dos días' means 'I have two days left.' It works the same way as 'gustar' in structure.

demo.context: A: Is there any coffee left?
demo.native: There are only a few drops left.

practice.context: Adam: Are there any tickets left for tonight?

usecases.name: Fit / suit clothing
usecases.explanation: When talking about how clothing fits or looks on someone, use 'quedar' with an indirect object pronoun. 'Me queda bien' = 'it fits me well.' The subject is the clothing, not the person — same structure as 'gustar.'

demo.context: A: How does that dress look on you?
demo.native: It fits me perfectly.

practice.context: Adam: What did you think of those jeans?
practice.english: They're a bit small on me.

usecases.name: Turn out / end up
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedar bien' or 'quedar mal' to describe how something turned out. A plan can 'quedar bien' (turn out well) or your food can 'quedar salado' (come out salty). Also works for reputation: 'quedé mal' = 'I came across badly.'

demo.context: A: How did the photos from the trip come out?
demo.native: They came out beautiful.

practice.context: Adam: What did everyone think of the presentation?

usecases.name: Be located
usecases.explanation: In Latin American Spanish, 'quedar' is commonly used to say where a place is located — like 'está' but specifically for fixed locations. 'Queda a dos cuadras' = 'it's two blocks away.' You'll hear this constantly when asking for directions.

demo.context: A: Is the pharmacy far from here?
demo.native: No, it's only two blocks away.

practice.context: Adam: Where's the nearest ATM?

usecases.name: Stay / remain in place
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedarse' (reflexive) when someone stays put or doesn't go somewhere. 'Me quedo' = 'I'm staying.' The reflexive form emphasizes the person is remaining while others move or go out.

demo.context: A: Are you going to the party tonight?
demo.native: No, I'm staying home.

practice.context: Adam: You should come with us!

usecases.name: Keep / take something
usecases.explanation: 'Quedarse con' means to keep something or choose one option for yourself. It's how you say 'I'll take this one' when choosing, or 'he kept my umbrella.' Think of it as keeping something in your possession.

demo.context: A: Which one do you want?
demo.native: I'll take the black one.

practice.context: Adam: Did she return your jacket?

usecases.name: Become (state change)
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedarse' + adjective when someone ends up in a lasting state — often involuntarily. Unlike 'ponerse' (sudden feeling), 'quedarse' implies being left in that state: 'quedarse dormido' (fall asleep), 'quedarse sin palabras' (be speechless).

demo.context: A: Why didn't you answer your phone?
demo.native: I fell asleep on the couch.
demo.spanish: Me quedé dormido en el sofá.

practice.context: Adam: What happened when she heard the news?

usecases.name: Quedar debiendo (to owe)
usecases.explanation: Use 'quedar debiendo' to say someone still owes something after an interaction — more specific than just 'deber.' 'Me quedó debiendo' = 'he still owes me.' Common after splitting bills or lending money.

demo.context: A: Did he ever pay you back?
demo.native: No, he still owes me.

practice.context: Adam: Hey, I thought you were going to pay for the coffee.

sentences.context: Adam: Did you hang out with anyone yesterday?
Adam: What time are you guys planning to meet tomorrow?
Adam: So what did you guys decide in the end?
Adam: Did you come to any conclusion or not?
Adam: Are there any slices of pizza left?
Adam: After buying all that stuff, how much cash do you have now?
Adam: How does that jacket fit you?
Adam: What about those pants you bought?

hints.note: 'too big on me' → just 'grandes' with quedar

sentences.context: Adam: Do you like how that hat looks on me?
Adam: How did the picture come out?
Adam: How did everything go at the event?
Adam: What happened with your test?
Adam: Is that new restaurant far from here?
Adam: Where exactly is that gym?
Adam: Hey, you still haven't paid me back, right?
Adam: Which one do you want to choose?
Adam: What happened with the extra cash?
Adam: Are you going out tonight?
Adam: Should I wait here or go with you?
Adam: Why didn't you answer my call last night?
Adam: How did she react when she heard the news?

dar.description: to give; dar igual/ganas/miedo/asco; darse cuenta/prisa

usecases.name: Give / pass something
usecases.explanation: The core meaning of 'dar' is to give or hand something to someone. The receiver gets an indirect object pronoun: 'te doy' = 'I give you,' 'le di' = 'I gave him/her.' Use it for gifts, passing objects, or abstract things like advice.

demo.context: A: I need to write down your contact.
demo.native: I'll give you my number.

practice.context: Adam: I need to sign that document.

usecases.name: Dar igual / Da lo mismo
usecases.explanation: 'Me da igual' and 'da lo mismo' both mean 'it doesn't matter' or 'it's all the same to me.' Use these when you genuinely have no preference. They're the go-to expressions for saying you don't care — more natural than 'no me importa' in casual conversation.

demo.context: A: Should we have pizza or tacos?
demo.native: Either is fine with me.

practice.context: Adam: Do you want to go at 7 or 8?
practice.english: It doesn't matter / It's all the same.

usecases.name: Dar ganas de
usecases.explanation: 'Dar ganas de' + infinitive means to feel like doing something — a sudden impulse or desire. 'Me dan ganas de salir' = 'I feel like going out.' The subject is the thing causing the feeling — similar structure to 'gustar.'

demo.context: A: It's such a perfect day outside.
demo.native: Makes me want to go to the beach.
demo.spanish: Me dan ganas de ir a la playa.

practice.context: Adam: This song is so good.
practice.english: It makes me want to dance.

usecases.name: Dar miedo / asco / pena
usecases.explanation: Use 'dar' + emotion noun to describe how something makes you feel: 'dar miedo' = to scare, 'dar asco' = to gross out, 'dar pena' = to make you feel embarrassed or bad. The thing causing the feeling is the subject: 'me da miedo' = 'it scares me.'

demo.context: A: Have you seen those huge spiders?
demo.native: They freak me out.

practice.context: Adam: Are you going to speak in front of everyone?
practice.english: It scares me / makes me nervous.

usecases.name: Darse cuenta
usecases.explanation: 'Darse cuenta de' means to realize or become aware of something. This is the standard everyday expression for realization — not 'realizar,' which usually means 'to carry out' in Spanish. You can drop 'de' in short constructions: '¿te diste cuenta?' = 'did you notice?'

demo.context: A: Why didn't you say something sooner?
demo.native: I didn't even realize.

practice.context: Adam: Did you see what just happened?

usecases.name: Darse prisa
usecases.explanation: 'Date prisa' means 'hurry up.' This is a fixed expression. You can also say 'tengo prisa' = 'I'm in a hurry' when you're the rushed one. Use 'date prisa' to tell someone else to get moving.

demo.context: A: The movie starts in 5 minutes!
demo.native: Hurry up, we're going to be late.

practice.context: Adam: Everyone's waiting — come on!

usecases.name: Dar una vuelta
usecases.explanation: 'Dar una vuelta' literally means 'to give a spin' but it's used for going for a walk, drive, or heading out for a bit. It's casual and vague — perfect for 'let's get out of the house' without specifying exactly what you're doing.

demo.context: A: You seem stressed. Have you been inside all day?
demo.native: I'm going to go for a quick walk.

practice.context: Adam: It's so nice outside. Want to get some air?
practice.english: Yeah, let's go for a walk.
practice.spanish: Sí, vamos a dar una vuelta
accepted_translations: Sí, vamos a dar una vuelta

usecases.name: Darse por vencido
usecases.explanation: 'Darse por vencido' means to give up or admit defeat. 'Me doy por vencido' = 'I give up.' The literal meaning is 'to consider oneself beaten.' Use it when someone stops trying — not for giving up on a plan (that's more like 'abandonar').

demo.context: A: You've been stuck on that for an hour. Are you stopping?
demo.native: I'm going to give up and ask for help.
demo.spanish: Me voy a dar por vencido y pedir ayuda.

practice.context: Adam: This is impossible. I'm done.

usecases.name: Dar resultado
usecases.explanation: 'Dar resultado' means that something worked, paid off, or produced results. The subject is the thing that worked (or didn't): 'dio resultado' = 'it worked out.' Use it to evaluate whether a method, plan, or effort was effective.

demo.context: A: Did the new approach end up working?
demo.native: Yes, it paid off.

practice.context: Adam: What happened with that strategy you tried?

usecases.name: Dar en el clavo
usecases.explanation: 'Dar en el clavo' is a fixed idiom meaning to get something exactly right — 'you nailed it' or 'you hit the nail on the head.' Use it when someone says or does exactly the right thing. Always in second/third person: 'diste en el clavo' = 'you nailed it.'

demo.context: A: I think the problem was that we didn't listen.
demo.native: You hit the nail on the head.

practice.context: Adam: You said exactly what I was thinking.

usecases.name: Darse un golpe (to hit yourself)
usecases.explanation: 'Darse un golpe' means to accidentally hit or bump yourself. 'Me di un golpe en la cabeza' = 'I hit my head.' It's the natural phrase for any accidental knock or bump against something.

demo.context: A: Why are you rubbing your elbow?
demo.native: I bumped it on the door.
demo.spanish: Me di un golpe en la puerta.

practice.context: Adam: What happened? Are you okay?
practice.spanish: Me di un golpe en la cabeza
accepted_translations: Me di un golpe en la cabeza

sentences.context: Adam: Could you hand something over at the table?
sentences.english: Can you pass me the water?

sentences.context: Adam: You mentioned you wanted to stay in touch.
Adam: Would you rather go to the beach or the mountains?
sentences.english: It doesn't matter to me / I don't care

sentences.context: Adam: Should we go at 6 or 7?
Adam: It's such a nice evening. What are you thinking?
Adam: Have you seen that horror movie yet?
Adam: What do you think of that smell?
Adam: Why didn't you say something sooner?
Adam: Did you see what just happened?
sentences.english: Did you notice? / Did you realize?

sentences.context: Adam: We're going to miss the bus!

sentences.category: Dar una vuelta (go for a walk/ride)
sentences.context: Adam: The weather is perfect outside today.
sentences.english: Want to go for a walk?

sentences.context: Adam: How did you feel when that happened in front of everyone?
sentences.english: I feel really bad / I'm so embarrassed

sentences.context: Adam: My friend helped you out, right?
sentences.spanish: Dale las gracias de mi parte
accepted_translations: Dale las gracias de mi parte

sentences.context: Adam: This is really hard. Are you going to stop trying?
Adam: Why are you limping?
sentences.spanish: Me di un golpe en la rodilla
accepted_translations: Me di un golpe en la rodilla

sentences.context: Adam: Why don't you ever speak up in meetings?

sentences.category: Dar resultado (to work out / pay off)
sentences.context: Adam: Did the new strategy end up working?

hints.note: 'dio' = past tense of dar, no accent on monosyllable

sentences.context: Adam: I haven't seen you in so long!
Adam: We should keep in touch.
sentences.english: Can you give me your number?

sentences.category: Dar en el clavo (to nail it)
sentences.context: Adam: You figured out exactly what the problem was!
sentences.english: You hit the nail on the head / You nailed it

hints.native: you hit the nail on the head

pasar.description: to happen, pass, spend time; pasarlo bien/mal, pasar de largo, no pasa nada

usecases.name: ¿Qué pasó? / ¿Qué pasa?
explanation_bullets: Both are extremely natural and you'll use them constantly
usecases.explanation: These are the two most common questions for asking what happened or what's going on. '¿Qué pasó?' = 'what happened?' (past). '¿Qué pasa?' = 'what's going on?' (present). Both are extremely natural and you'll use them constantly.

demo.context: A: [Someone looks stressed]
demo.native: What's going on?

practice.context: Adam: You look really upset. Is everything okay?

usecases.name: No pasa nada

explanation_bullets.text: Use this to say "no worries" or "it's okay" or "don't worry about it"

explanation_bullets: It literally means 'nothing is happening' — i.e. there's no problem
You'll use it constantly when someone apologizes for something minor.
usecases.explanation: 'No pasa nada' is the go-to response for 'no worries,' 'it's okay,' or 'don't worry about it.' It literally means 'nothing is happening' — i.e., there's no problem. You'll use it constantly when someone apologizes for something minor.

demo.context: A: I'm so sorry I forgot to text you back!
demo.native: Don't worry about it.

practice.context: Adam: I spilled coffee on your notes. I'm really sorry.

usecases.name: Pasarlo bien / mal

explanation_bullets.text: Use this to say "I had a great time"

explanation_bullets: The 'lo' or 'la' refers to the time spent.
The opposite 'pasarlo mal' = to have a rough time or bad experience.
usecases.explanation: 'Pasarla bien' or 'pasarlo bien' is how you say you had a good time. 'Lo pasé muy bien' = 'I had a great time.' The 'lo' or 'la' refers to the time spent. The opposite 'pasarlo mal' = to have a rough time or bad experience.

demo.context: A: How was your birthday party?
demo.native: We had such a good time!

practice.context: Adam: How was the weekend trip?

usecases.name: Pasar por (pick up)
explanation_bullets: 'Pasar por alguien' means to pick someone up or stop by to get them.
It's the everyday way to say you'll swing by for someone.

explanation_bullets.text: You can say "I'll come get you / I'll pick you up."

usecases.explanation: 'Pasar por alguien' or 'pasar a buscar a alguien' means to pick someone up or stop by to get them. It's the everyday way to say you'll swing by for someone. 'Paso por ti' = 'I'll come get you / I'll pick you up.'

demo.context: A: How are you getting to the concert?
demo.native: My friend is picking me up.

practice.context: Adam: How should I get there?
practice.english: I'll come pick you up at 8.
practice.spanish: Paso por ti a las ocho
accepted_translations: Paso por ti a las ocho

usecases.name: Pasar de largo
explanation_bullets: 'Pasar de largo' means to go past something or someone without stopping — like walking right by a store you meant to enter.
It can also mean to ignore someone on purpose.
The key idea is moving past without pausing.
usecases.explanation: 'Pasar de largo' means to go past something or someone without stopping — like walking right by a store you meant to enter. It can also mean to ignore someone on purpose. The key idea is moving past without pausing.

demo.context: A: Did you stop at the bakery?
demo.native: I walked right past it without noticing.
demo.spanish: Pasé de largo sin darme cuenta.

practice.context: Adam: Did you stop by my office earlier?

usecases.name: Pasarse
explanation_bullets: 'Pasarse' means to go too far, overdo it, or cross a line.
'Me pasé' can mean you ate too much, worked too hard, or said something inappropriate.
It covers any kind of excess where you went beyond what was reasonable.
usecases.explanation: 'Pasarse' means to go too far, overdo it, or cross a line. 'Me pasé' can mean you ate too much, worked too hard, or said something inappropriate. It covers any kind of excess where you went beyond what was reasonable.

demo.context: A: You look exhausted.
demo.native: Yeah, I overdid it at the gym.
demo.spanish: Sí, me pasé en el gimnasio.

practice.context: Adam: You ate like five tacos!

usecases.name: Pasa que...
explanation_bullets: 'Pasa que' is how you introduce an explanation for something unexpected — like 'the thing is...' or 'here's the deal...'
Use it when you're about to explain a situation.
It signals that a reason or context is coming.
usecases.explanation: 'Pasa que' is how you introduce an explanation for something unexpected — like 'the thing is...' or 'here's the deal...' Use it when you're about to explain a situation. It signals that a reason or context is coming.

demo.context: A: Why are you home so early?
demo.native: The thing is, the event was cancelled.

practice.context: Adam: I thought you were coming to dinner tonight?
practice.english: The thing is, I completely forgot.
practice.spanish: Pasa que se me olvidó por completo
accepted_translations: Pasa que se me olvidó por completo
Pasa que se me olvido por completo

usecases.name: Pasar tiempo / el rato
explanation_bullets: 'Pasar tiempo' means to spend time.
'Pasar el rato' is more casual — to hang out or kill time without a specific plan.
Both are very common when describing how you spent your day or talking about being with someone.
usecases.explanation: 'Pasar tiempo' means to spend time. 'Pasar el rato' is more casual — to hang out or kill time without a specific plan. Both are very common when describing how you spent your day or talking about being with someone.

demo.context: A: Are you two close friends?
demo.native: Yeah, we spend a lot of time together.

practice.context: Adam: What are you guys doing right now?
practice.english: We're just hanging out / killing time.

usecases.name: Pasar por alto
explanation_bullets: 'Pasar por alto' means to overlook or miss something — whether accidentally or intentionally.
Use it for mistakes you didn't catch, details you ignored, or things you chose to let slide.

explanation_bullets.text: Like "I overlooked it / I missed it."

usecases.explanation: 'Pasar por alto' means to overlook or miss something — whether accidentally or intentionally. Use it for mistakes you didn't catch, details you ignored, or things you chose to let slide. 'Lo pasé por alto' = 'I overlooked it / I missed it.'

demo.context: A: There's a big typo on the first page.
demo.native: I completely missed it.

practice.context: Adam: Did you catch the error before sending?

usecases.name: ¿Te pasó algo?
explanation_bullets: '¿Te pasó algo?' is the personal, concerned version of '¿Qué pasó?' — you use it when you're worried about a specific person.

explanation_bullets.text: It means "did something happen to you?" or "are you okay?"

explanation_bullets: It's more intimate and shows concern.
usecases.explanation: '¿Te pasó algo?' is the personal, concerned version of '¿Qué pasó?' — you use it when you're worried about a specific person. It means 'did something happen to you?' or 'are you okay?' It's more intimate and shows concern.

demo.context: A: [A friend seems distracted and sad]
demo.native: Are you okay? Did something happen?

practice.context: Adam: You've been really quiet today.
practice.english: Is everything okay? Did something happen?

usecases.name: ¡Pasa!
explanation_bullets: ¡Pasa! is the informal imperative of pasar — the everyday way to say come in or go ahead.
You will hear it when someone answers the door (¡Pasa, pasa!) or waves you through.
The formal version is ¡Pase!.
It signals an open invitation to enter or proceed.
usecases.explanation: ¡Pasa! is the informal imperative of pasar — the everyday way to say come in or go ahead. You will hear it when someone answers the door (¡Pasa, pasa!) or waves you through. The formal version is ¡Pase!. It signals an open invitation to enter or proceed.

demo.context: A: *knocks on the door*
demo.native: Come in! / Come on in!

practice.context: Sofía: There is a knock at the door. You invite whoever it is to come in.

usecases.name: Pasar frío
explanation_bullets: Pasar frío means to be cold or endure cold — not just feeling cold in the moment (that is tengo frío) but experiencing it over time.
The same pattern works for heat: pasar calor.
Think of pasar here as to go through a physical condition.
usecases.explanation: Pasar frío means to be cold or endure cold — not just feeling cold in the moment (that is tengo frío) but experiencing it over time. The same pattern works for heat: pasar calor. Think of pasar here as to go through a physical condition.

demo.context: A: Was it cold at the beach last night?
demo.native: Yeah, we were freezing.

practice.context: Sara: How was sleeping outside? Was it cold?

usecases.name: Pasar la noche
explanation_bullets: Pasar la noche means to spend the night somewhere.
It is more specific than quedarse — it emphasizes the full overnight stay.
Use it to say you slept over, stayed the night at a place, or were somewhere all night.
usecases.explanation: Pasar la noche means to spend the night somewhere. It is more specific than quedarse — it emphasizes the full overnight stay. Use it to say you slept over, stayed the night at a place, or were somewhere all night.

demo.context: A: Did you drive back after the party?
demo.native: No, I spent the night there.

practice.context: Miguel: Where did you sleep after the concert?
practice.english: I spent the night at Sofía's place.
practice.spanish: Pasé la noche en casa de Sofía
accepted_translations: Pasé la noche en casa de Sofía
Pase la noche en casa de Sofia
Pasé la noche en casa de Sofia
Pase la noche en casa de Sofía

usecases.name: Pasar un momento difícil
usecases.english: to go through a hard time
explanation_bullets: Pasar un momento difícil means to go through a hard time.
Pasar here means to experience or endure — the same verb that powers pasarlo bien/mal.
This phrase fits serious situations: grief, illness, a tough period in life.
You can also say pasar por un momento difícil.
usecases.explanation: Pasar un momento difícil means to go through a hard time. Pasar here means to experience or endure — the same verb that powers pasarlo bien/mal. This phrase fits serious situations: grief, illness, a tough period in life. You can also say pasar por un momento difícil.

demo.context: A: How is your friend doing after the breakup?
demo.native: She is going through a really hard time.
demo.spanish: Está pasando un momento muy difícil.

practice.context: Luis: Your colleague just lost their job. How are they holding up?
practice.english: He went through a really hard time.
accepted_translations: Pasó por un momento muy difícil
Paso por un momento muy dificil

sentences.context: Adam: You look upset. Is everything okay?
Adam: You walk in and everyone looks tense.
Adam: You seem really off today.
sentences.english: What's the matter with you? / What's wrong?

sentences.context: Adam: Sorry, I accidentally knocked your drink over.
Adam: How was the party last night?
Adam: How was the trip with your friends?
Adam: Should I meet you there or what?
sentences.spanish: Paso por ti a las siete
accepted_translations: Paso por ti a las siete

sentences.context: Adam: Someone knocks at the door.

sentences.category: Pasar de largo (go past without stopping)
sentences.context: Adam: Did you find the coffee shop?
sentences.english: I walked right past it / I missed it

sentences.category: Pasarse (to overdo it / go too far)
sentences.context: Adam: You ate like three plates. How do you feel?
sentences.english: I overdid it / I went too far

sentences.context: Adam: Why weren't you at the meeting?
Adam: I heard things didn't go well for you.
sentences.english: I had a rough time / It was really hard

sentences.context: Adam: Are you two close?
sentences.english: We spent a lot of time together

sentences.context: Adam: Did you drive back that same night?
Adam: How was the camping trip? Was it cold at night?
sentences.english: We were really cold / We froze

sentences.context: Adam: How did that mistake slip through?
sentences.english: I overlooked it / I missed it

sentences.category: Pasar el rato (hang out / kill time)
sentences.context: Adam: What are you guys doing right now?
sentences.english: We're just hanging out / killing time

sentences.context: Adam: How are we getting there?
Adam: You seemed really stressed last month.
sentences.english: I went through a really hard time

sentences.context: Adam: You look like something's wrong.
sentences.english: Did something happen? / Are you okay?

volver.description: to return/come back; volver a = do again; volverse = to become

usecases.name: Come back / return
usecases.brief: The standard verb for physically returning somewhere.
explanation_bullets: Use for returning home, from a trip, or to a conversation
Vuelvo ahora → I'll be right back
¿Cuándo vuelves? → When are you coming back?
usecases.explanation: 'Volver' is the standard verb for coming back or returning somewhere. 'Vuelvo ahora' = 'I'll be right back.' '¿Cuándo vuelves?' = 'when are you coming back?' Use it for returning home, from a trip, or to a conversation.

demo.context: A: Where are you going?
demo.native: I'll be right back.

practice.context: Adam: She's going on a trip. When does she get back?

usecases.name: Volver a + infinitive
usecases.brief: Add 'a + verb' after volver to mean 'do it again.'
explanation_bullets: Volver a + infinitive = to do something again
Lo volvió a hacer → He did it again
More natural than 'hacer otra vez' in most contexts
Object pronoun goes before volver: la volví a llamar
usecases.explanation: 'Volver a' + infinitive means 'to do something again.' 'Lo volvió a hacer' = 'he did it again.' This is one of the most useful constructions in Spanish — more natural than 'hacer otra vez' in most contexts.

demo.context: A: I thought you quit smoking.
demo.native: He started again.

practice.context: Adam: Did you try calling her?

usecases.name: No volver a
usecases.brief: The strong way to say 'I won't do it again.'
explanation_bullets: No + volver a + inf = won't do it again / never again
No lo vuelvo a hacer → I won't do it again
Stronger commitment or warning than 'no lo hago de nuevo'
usecases.explanation: The negative 'no volver a' means 'won't do again' or 'never again.' 'No lo vuelvo a hacer' = 'I won't do it again.' This is a strong and common way to make a commitment or give a warning.

demo.context: A: You were an hour late again!
demo.native: It won't happen again, I promise.
demo.spanish: No vuelve a pasar, te lo juro.

practice.context: Adam: Did you patch things up with him?
practice.english: No, I never want to see him again.
practice.spanish: No, no quiero volver a verlo
accepted_translations: No, no quiero volver a verlo

hints.native: I never want to see him again

usecases.name: Volverse + adjective
usecases.brief: 'Volverse' describes a gradual, lasting change in someone.
explanation_bullets: Se volvió + adj = someone became a certain way (lasting change)
Se volvió muy distante → He became very distant
Use this, not 'ponerse,' when the change is gradual or permanent
usecases.explanation: 'Volverse' + adjective means someone became a certain way — often describing a gradual personality change. 'Se volvió muy distante' = 'he became very distant.' It implies a more lasting change than 'ponerse' (which is more sudden and emotional).

demo.context: A: Is Marco the same as before?
demo.native: He's become much more serious.
demo.spanish: Se ha vuelto mucho más serio.

practice.context: Adam: What happened to her after moving abroad?

usecases.name: Volverse loco
usecases.brief: 'Going crazy' — can be dramatic or affectionate.
explanation_bullets: Me estoy volviendo loco → I'm losing my mind
Me vuelves loco → You drive me crazy (as a compliment)
Works in 1st and 3rd person
usecases.explanation: 'Volverse loco' is a very common expression for going crazy, losing your mind, or being driven mad. It can be dramatic ('I'm overwhelmed') or affectionate: 'me vuelves loco' = 'you drive me crazy' as a compliment.

demo.context: A: How's work been lately?
demo.native: Crazy. I'm losing my mind.

practice.context: Adam: There's so much noise — I can't concentrate.

usecases.name: Volver en sí
usecases.brief: Fixed phrase: to regain consciousness after fainting.
explanation_bullets: Volver en sí = to come to / regain consciousness
Always reflexive: volvió en sí → he came to
Mainly used in dramatic or medical contexts
usecases.explanation: 'Volver en sí' is a fixed expression meaning to regain consciousness or come to after fainting. Always used with a reflexive pronoun: 'volvió en sí' = 'he came to.' You'll mainly encounter this in dramatic or medical contexts.

demo.context: A: What happened after she fainted?
demo.native: She came to after about a minute.
demo.spanish: Volvió en sí al cabo de un minuto.

practice.context: Adam: He passed out — is he okay?

usecases.name: Volver a empezar
usecases.brief: 'To start over from scratch' — more natural than 'comenzar de nuevo.'
explanation_bullets: Volver a empezar = to start over
Hay que volver a empezar → We have to start over
More conversational than 'comenzar de nuevo'
usecases.explanation: 'Volver a empezar' means to start over from scratch. Combine with 'hay que' for 'we have to start over.' More natural in conversation than 'comenzar de nuevo.' Use it whenever a plan falls apart and you need to begin again.

demo.context: A: The whole project got deleted.
demo.native: We're going to have to start over.
demo.spanish: Vamos a tener que volver a empezar.

practice.context: Adam: That design doesn't work at all. What now?

usecases.name: ¿Ya volvió?
usecases.brief: Use 'ya' to ask or confirm someone is back.
explanation_bullets: Ya volvió → He/she is already back
¿Ya volvió? → Is he/she back yet?
'Ya' marks completed action — you'll use this constantly
usecases.explanation: 'Ya volvió' = 'he/she is already back.' The question '¿Ya volvió?' = 'is he/she back yet?' The word 'ya' marks completed action or asks about it — you'll use this constantly when waiting for someone.

demo.context: A: Is your mom home?
demo.native: She's not back yet.

practice.context: Adam: I've been waiting for Carlos for 20 minutes.

sentences.context: Adam: Where are you going?
Adam: You're heading out on a trip tomorrow?
Adam: What time did you get home last night?
Adam: I'm leaving now, see you next time!
Adam: I told him not to do that. Did he listen?

hints.note: volver a + verb = to do something again

sentences.context: Adam: This is the third time this week!
Adam: You made a mistake. Will you do it again?
Adam: That didn't work out. What now?
Adam: What's going on with you and her?
sentences.english: When are you seeing her again?

sentences.context: Adam: What time did everyone get back from the concert?
Adam: How's work been lately?
sentences.english: I'm going crazy / losing my mind

sentences.context: Adam: He used to be so laid-back. What happened?
Adam: He keeps doing things that bother you.
Adam: What happened after he fainted?
sentences.english: He came to / He regained consciousness

sentences.context: Adam: The whole plan fell through. What are we doing?

sentences.category: Come back / return (go back to topic)
sentences.context: Adam: We got sidetracked. What were we talking about?
sentences.english: Let's go back to that topic

sentences.context: Adam: I heard you two had a falling out.
sentences.english: Did you talk to him again?

hints.native: did you talk to him again

sentences.category: No volver a (don't want to see again)
sentences.context: Adam: Are you going to patch things up with him?
sentences.english: I don't want to see him again

hints.native: I don't want to see him again

sentences.context: Adam: After everything he went through, how is he now?
sentences.english: He was back to his old self
sentences.spanish: Volvió a ser el mismo de siempre
accepted_translations: Volvió a ser el mismo de siempre
Volvio a ser el mismo de siempre

hints.learning: volvió a ser el mismo de siempre

sentences.context: Adam: I've been waiting for her. Any sign of her?

poner.description: to put/place, turn on media; ponerse = put on clothing, to become

usecases.name: Put on media
usecases.explanation: 'Poner' is the standard verb for putting on music, TV, a movie, or any media. 'Pon música' = 'put on some music.' '¿Ponemos una peli?' = 'should we put on a movie?' Much more natural than 'escuchar' when you're choosing to play something.

demo.context: A: What should we listen to?
demo.native: I'll put on a playlist.

practice.context: Adam: It's too quiet in here.

usecases.name: Ponerse ropa
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse' (reflexive) means to put on clothing or accessories. 'Ponte el abrigo' = 'put on your coat.' In Spanish, you use the definite article instead of the possessive with clothing: 'ponte los zapatos' = 'put on your shoes.'

demo.context: A: It's freezing outside.
demo.native: Put on a jacket.

practice.context: Adam: We're going out and it's cold.
practice.english: I need to put on my shoes.

usecases.name: Ponerse de acuerdo
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse de acuerdo' means to agree on something or get on the same page as a group. 'Hay que ponernos de acuerdo' = 'we need to sort this out together.' It implies a negotiation — not just saying 'estoy de acuerdo' (I agree with you).

demo.context: A: Everyone keeps making different decisions.
demo.native: We need to get on the same page.

practice.context: Adam: There are too many different opinions here.
practice.english: We need to agree on something.

usecases.name: Ponerse + adjective
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse' + adjective describes a sudden or emotional change of state. 'Me puse nervioso' = 'I got nervous,' 'se puso contento' = 'he got happy.' Unlike 'volverse' (gradual personality change), 'ponerse' is for feelings and reactions that happen in the moment.

demo.context: A: Did she like the surprise?
demo.native: She got really emotional.

practice.context: Adam: How were you before the job interview?

usecases.name: Ponerse a + infinitive
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse a' + infinitive means to suddenly start doing something, often in reaction to something else. 'Se puso a llorar' = 'she started crying.' 'Me puse a trabajar' = 'I got to work.' It conveys diving abruptly into an activity.

demo.context: A: What happened when you told them the news?
demo.native: They started laughing.

practice.context: Adam: What did you do when it started raining?

usecases.name: Poner de mal humor
usecases.explanation: 'Poner de mal humor' means to put someone in a bad mood. The thing causing the mood is the subject: 'me pone de mal humor' = 'it puts me in a bad mood.' Similarly, 'poner de buen humor' = 'to put in a good mood.'

demo.context: A: Why do you hate that sound so much?
demo.native: It just puts me in a bad mood instantly.
demo.spanish: Me pone de mal humor al instante.

practice.context: Adam: Why are you upset when they cancel plans?
practice.english: It puts me in a bad mood.

hints.native: puts me in a bad mood

usecases.name: Poner al día
usecases.explanation: 'Poner al día' means to fill someone in or bring them up to speed on what they missed. 'Ponme al día' = 'fill me in / catch me up.' 'Te pongo al día' = 'I'll fill you in.' Very common when returning after an absence.

demo.context: A: I missed the whole meeting.
demo.native: Let me fill you in.

practice.context: Adam: What happened while I was on vacation?
practice.english: Fill me in / Catch me up.

hints.native: fill me in / catch me up

usecases.name: Ponerse en contacto
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse en contacto' means to get in touch with someone or reach out. 'Me pondré en contacto contigo' = 'I'll get in touch with you.' More complete than just 'te llamo' when you're not sure of the method — professional and natural.

demo.context: A: How should I reach the manager?
demo.native: He'll get in touch with you tomorrow.
demo.spanish: Se pondrá en contacto contigo mañana.

practice.context: Adam: I need to talk to you about something important.
practice.english: I'll get in touch with you later.
practice.spanish: Me pondré en contacto contigo más tarde
accepted_translations: Me pondré en contacto contigo más tarde
Me pondre en contacto contigo más tarde
Me pondré en contacto contigo mas tarde
Me pondre en contacto contigo mas tarde

usecases.name: Ponerse en el lugar
usecases.explanation: 'Ponerse en el lugar de alguien' means to put yourself in someone else's shoes — to see things from their perspective. 'Ponte en mi lugar' = 'put yourself in my shoes.' Use it when asking for empathy or understanding.

demo.context: A: Why do you think she reacted like that?
demo.native: Try to put yourself in her shoes.

practice.context: Adam: I don't see why that would bother you.

usecases.name: Poner en práctica
usecases.explanation: 'Poner en práctica' means to put something into practice — to actually apply what you've learned or planned. 'Vamos a poner esto en práctica' = 'let's put this into practice.' It's the bridge between knowing something and actually doing it.

demo.context: A: We've been talking about this for months.
demo.native: It's time to put it into practice.
demo.spanish: Es hora de ponerlo en práctica.

practice.context: Adam: We finally have a strategy. Now what?
practice.english: Now we put it into practice.

usecases.name: Poner (put / place)
usecases.explanation: The core meaning of 'poner' is to put or place something somewhere. 'Ponlo en la mesa' = 'put it on the table.' It's the default verb for physically placing objects — more active than 'estar' or 'quedar.'

demo.context: A: Where should I put the groceries?
demo.native: Just put them on the counter.

practice.context: Adam: Where do you want this?

usecases.name: Poner a cargo (put in charge)
usecases.explanation: Use 'poner a alguien a cargo' to say someone is being given responsibility or authority. 'Me pusieron a cargo' = 'they put me in charge.' It implies a formal assignment — much more natural than 'hacer responsable.'

demo.context: A: Who's running the project now?
demo.native: They put María in charge.

practice.context: Adam: Who's going to lead the new team?

usecases.name: Poner la mesa / excusas / atención
usecases.explanation: 'Poner' appears in several fixed collocations: 'poner la mesa' (set the table), 'poner excusas' (make excuses), 'poner atención' (pay attention). Just like English has 'set the table' and 'make excuses,' Spanish uses 'poner' for all three.

demo.context: A: Help me get dinner ready.
demo.native: I'll set the table.

practice.context: Adam: Stop coming up with reasons. Just do it.

usecases.name: ¿Qué le pongo? (What can I get you?)
usecases.explanation: When a server asks what you'd like to order, they say '¿Qué le pongo?' — literally 'what shall I put for you?' This is the standard server phrase across Latin America, far more common than '¿Qué quiere ordenar?' in casual settings.

demo.context: A: [Server approaches your table]
demo.native: What can I get you?

practice.context: Adam: [You're the server. A customer sits down.]

sentences.context: Adam: It's quiet in here. Can we liven it up?
Adam: You're at a friend's place and want to watch something.
sentences.english: Can I put on the TV?

sentences.context: Adam: It's cold outside. You should bundle up.
Adam: We keep making different decisions. This is a mess.
sentences.english: We need to get on the same page / agree

sentences.context: Adam: You keep getting distracted.
Adam: Did he follow through on what he said?
Adam: How were you before your big presentation?
Adam: How did he react when you told him the good news?
sentences.english: He was really happy / He lit up

sentences.context: Adam: Why do you hate that song so much?
sentences.english: It puts me in a bad mood

sentences.context: Adam: You missed a week. How did you catch up?
sentences.english: He filled me in / got me up to speed

hints.native: filled me in / up to speed

sentences.context: Adam: Where did you leave your stuff?
Adam: You're moving into a new place and rearranging.
sentences.english: I don't know where to put this

sentences.context: Adam: Who's running the new project?

sentences.category: Ponerse en el lugar (put yourself in my shoes)
sentences.context: Adam: I don't think you understand how I feel.
Adam: Can your manager reach out to me directly?
sentences.english: I'll get in touch with you

sentences.context: Adam: We talked about new ideas. Now what?
sentences.english: We need to put that into practice
sentences.spanish: Hay que poner eso en práctica
accepted_translations: Hay que poner eso en práctica
Hay que poner eso en practica

sentences.context: Adam: Dinner's almost ready. Can someone help out?

sentences.category: ¿Qué le pongo? (What can I get you?)
sentences.context: Adam: You're a waiter approaching a customer's table.
Adam: What happened when you told her the bad news?

hints.note: ponerse a + verb = to start doing

sentences.context: Adam: What happened when the speaker walked in?
